                        1

                    Check name:
                        Stage Variables Implementation

                    Explanation:
                        Stage variable "SK" must create a new surrogate key only when "lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_ID" is null. The PySpark code ignores this condition and calls SurrogateKeyGen whenever "ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK" is null. If a lookup row exists but its surrogate key is unexpectedly null, the DataStage logic returns null, whereas the current PySpark logic will generate a new key, leading to incorrect surrogate-key values.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            "StageVariables": [{"Name": "SK", "Expression": " if IsNull(lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_ID) = @TRUE then KeyMgtGetNextValueConcurrent((\"ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK\")) else lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK"}

                        Target content:
                            df_primarykey_cols = (
                                df_primarykey_join
                                .withColumn(
                                    "ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK",
                                    F.col("lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK")
                                )
                                ...
                            )
                            
                            # Rename to df_enriched and apply SurrogateKeyGen for column ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK
                            df_enriched = df_primarykey_cols
                            df_enriched = SurrogateKeyGen(df_enriched,<DB sequence name>,"ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK",<schema>,<secret_name>)


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Add an explicit conditional column that follows the DataStage rule:
                           df_primarykey_cols = df_primarykey_join.withColumn(
                               "ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK",
                               F.when(F.col("lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_ID").isNull(), F.lit(None))  # leave null if new
                                .otherwise(F.col("lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK"))
                           )
                        
                        2. Call SurrogateKeyGen ONLY for the rows where "lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_ID" is null:
                           df_enriched = SurrogateKeyGen(
                               df_primarykey_cols,
                               <DB sequence name>,
                               "ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_SK",
                               <schema>,
                               <secret_name>,
                               condition_col="lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_DSCRTN_ID"   # implement condition inside utility or filter rows before call
                           )
                           (If SurrogateKeyGen cannot take a condition, first split the dataframe into two: rows needing keys vs. rows having keys, run SurrogateKeyGen on the first set, then union back.)
                        
                        3. Remove the unconditional SurrogateKeyGen invocation so that existing surrogate keys from the lookup are preserved exactly as in DataStage.

--- Item 316 ---
    All stages passed:
        False

    Failed checks:
        --- Item 1 ---
            Passed all checks:
                False

            Failed checks:
                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        2

                    Check name:
                        SQL Query Translation

                    Explanation:
                        DataStage SQL performs a pure INSERT after a table-level TRUNCATE, while the PySpark code replaces this with a MERGE (UPDATE/INSERT) and omits the truncate.
                        
                        DataStage SQL (Input pin):
                          • "INSERT INTO #$IDSOwner#.W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT ... VALUES ..."
                          • TableAction="Truncate" (implies TRUNCATE TABLE before insert).
                        
                        PySpark code:
                          • Builds a staging table and executes a MERGE statement that updates existing rows ("WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE") instead of simply inserting new rows.
                          • No TRUNCATE TABLE command is issued before the write.
                        
                        This difference changes the behaviour: existing rows in W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT will be updated rather than the table being emptied and repopulated as in DataStage.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            "Sql": "INSERT INTO #$IDSOwner#.W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT (SRC_SYS_CD_SK,CLM_ID) VALUES (ORCHESTRATE.SRC_SYS_CD_SK,ORCHESTRATE.CLM_ID)
                            
                            CREATE TABLE #$IDSOwner#.W_PAYMT_SUM (
                            SRC_SYS_CD_SK INTEGER NOT NULL, 
                            PAYMT_REF_ID CHAR(16) NOT NULL, 
                            PAYMT_SUM_LOB_CD CHAR(4) NOT NULL
                             )
                            
                            DROP TABLE #$IDSOwner#.W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT","Connection_WriteMode": "Insert","TableAction": "Truncate"

                        Target content:
                            merge_sql_W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT = f"""
                            MERGE {IDSOwner}.W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT AS T
                            USING STAGING.IdsAltFundInvcPaymt_W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_temp AS S
                            ON T.SRC_SYS_CD_SK = S.SRC_SYS_CD_SK
                               AND T.CLM_ID = S.CLM_ID
                            WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET
                               T.SRC_SYS_CD_SK = S.SRC_SYS_CD_SK,
                               T.CLM_ID = S.CLM_ID
                            WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT
                               (SRC_SYS_CD_SK, CLM_ID)
                               VALUES (S.SRC_SYS_CD_SK, S.CLM_ID);
                            """
                            execute_dml(merge_sql_W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT, jdbc_url, jdbc_props)


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Add an explicit TRUNCATE statement before writing, to honour TableAction="Truncate":
                             execute_dml(f"TRUNCATE TABLE {IDSOwner}.W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT", jdbc_url, jdbc_props)
                        
                        2. Replace the MERGE with a straight INSERT that mirrors the DataStage logic:
                             execute_dml(f"INSERT INTO {IDSOwner}.W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT (SRC_SYS_CD_SK, CLM_ID)
                                          SELECT SRC_SYS_CD_SK, CLM_ID FROM STAGING.IdsAltFundInvcPaymt_W_ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_temp", jdbc_url, jdbc_props)
                           (No UPDATE clause).
                        
                        3. If idempotency is required, deduplicate the staging dataframe before the insert, but do not update target rows.
                        
                        4. Keep the existing CREATE TABLE W_PAYMT_SUM and DROP TABLE statements as they already match the DataStage SQL.
                        
                        Applying these steps will align the PySpark implementation with the intent of the original DataStage stage.

                        Source content:
                            {"InputPins": [{"LinkIdentifier": "V0S20P1", "LinkName": "Key", "LinkType": "PrimaryLink", "InputIdentifiers": "V0S11|V0S11P1", "InputStageName": "IdsAltFundInvcPaymt"}, {"LinkIdentifier": "V0S20P7", "LinkName": "ClmSkLkup", "LinkType": "LookupLink", "JoinType": "left", "InputIdentifiers": "V155S4|V155S4P2", "InputStageName": "hf_iafip_clm", "JoinConditions": [{"SourceKeyOrValue": "SrcSysCdSk", "LookupKey": "ClmSkLkup.SRC_SYS_CD_SK"}, {"SourceKeyOrValue": "Key.CLM_ID", "LookupKey": "ClmSkLkup.CLM_ID"}]}]}

                            )

                        2. Add that value to the primary dataframe before the join, e.g.:

                           ```
                           (or use the correct datatype).
                           –-OR-–  simply use the literal directly in the join condition.

                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        4

                    Check name:
                        Output Column Implementation - Expression Check

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage column BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT is defined with the expression:
                        FORMAT.DATE(Extract.BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT, "SYBASE", "TIMESTAMP", "CCYY-MM-DD")
                        which converts the incoming timestamp to a string with the pattern CCYY-MM-DD.
                        
                        In the PySpark translation the column is created with:
                        withColumn("BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT", F.col("BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT"))
                        which simply passes the source value through without any date-format conversion. Therefore the required transformation logic is missing.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            {"ColumnName": "BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT", "Expression": "FORMAT.DATE(Extract.BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT, \"SYBASE\", \"TIMESTAMP\", \"CCYY-MM-DD\")"}

                        Target content:
                                .withColumn("BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT", F.col("BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT"))


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Import the date-formatting functions if not already present, e.g.
                           from pyspark.sql import functions as F, types as T
                        
                        2. Replace the current line in the StripField transformer:
                           .withColumn("BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT", F.col("BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT"))
                        
                           with something that converts the timestamp column to a string in the format CCYY-MM-DD.  For example:
                           .withColumn(
                               "BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT",
                               F.date_format(F.col("BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT"), "yyyy-MM-dd")
                           )
                           or, if the Sybase TIMESTAMP value must first be cast, do
                           .withColumn(
                               "BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT",
                               F.date_format(F.col("BLPS_FNDG_FROM_DT").cast("timestamp"), "yyyy-MM-dd")
                           )
                        
                        3. Ensure the resulting column is a string (char/varchar) to mimic the DataStage FORMAT.DATE output.
                        
                        4. Run unit tests comparing a known timestamp such as 2023-02-15 08:30:00 to verify it converts to the string "2023-02-15".

        --- Item 3 ---

            Failed checks:
                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        1

                    Check name:
                        Stage Variables Implementation

                    Explanation:
                        Stage variable "SK" must contain the surrogate key that is generated when lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK IS NULL.  In the PySpark code `SK` is created *before* the call to `SurrogateKeyGen`, therefore it is equal to the (still-null) value in column `ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK`.  After `SurrogateKeyGen` populates that column with the new key, `SK` is **not** refreshed, so its value is wrong for all newly-inserted rows.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            {"Name": "SK", "Expression": " if IsNull(lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK) = @TRUE then KeyMgtGetNextValueConcurrent((\"IDS_SK\")) else lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK"}

                        Target content:
                            df_enriched = (
                                df_join
                                .withColumn(
                                    "ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK",
                                    F.when(F.isnull("lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK"), F.lit(None))
                                     .otherwise(F.col("lkup.ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK"))
                                )
                                ...
                                .withColumn("SK", F.col("ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK"))  # We'll finalize after SurrogateKeyGen
                            )
                            
                            # Now call SurrogateKeyGen to populate ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK if null
                            
                            df_enriched = SurrogateKeyGen(df_enriched,<DB sequence name>,"ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK",<schema>,<secret_name>)


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Remove the premature creation of column `SK` that appears **before** the `SurrogateKeyGen` call.
                        2. After the `SurrogateKeyGen` call, add a new statement so that `SK` is derived from the now-final column `ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK`:
                           ```python
                           df_enriched = SurrogateKeyGen(...)
                           df_enriched = df_enriched.withColumn("SK", F.col("ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK"))
                           ```
                        3. Make sure any downstream DataFrames (`df_IdsAltFundInvcPaymt`, `df_updt`) that reference `SK` are **after** this corrected statement.
                        4. Re-run unit tests to verify that rows with a previously NULL lookup key now receive a non-null surrogate key in both `SK` and `ALT_FUND_INVC_PAYMT_SK`.

                    Failed check index:
                        2

                    Check name:
                        SQL Query Translation

                    Explanation:
                        The placeholder "#CurrDate#" that appears in several DataStage queries is still present as a literal string in the PySpark f-strings (e.g., variable `extract_query_lnkSbsbMedCov`).
                        This means the query sent to the database will literally contain the text “#CurrDate#” rather than the runtime value held in the widget variable `CurrDate`, so the semantics of the DataStage stage are not reproduced.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            "Sql": "SELECT SBSB.SBSB_CK,
                                   SBSB.SBSB_ID,
                                   SGSG.SGSG_ID
                                   
                                
                              FROM #$FacetsOwner#.CMC_BLEI_ENTY_INFO BLEI,
                                   #$FacetsOwner#.CMC_MEME_MEMBER MEME, 
                                   #$FacetsOwner#.CMC_MEPE_PRCS_ELIG ELIG,
                                   #$FacetsOwner#.CMC_SBSB_SUBSC SBSB,
                                   #$FacetsOwner#.CMC_SGSG_SUB_GROUP SGSG
                            WHERE 
                                        BLEI.BLEI_BILL_LEVEL_CK    = SBSB.SBSB_CK 
                              AND  BLEI.BLEI_BILL_LEVEL           = 'I' 
                              AND   SBSB.SBSB_CK                      = MEME.SBSB_CK
                              AND   MEME.MEME_REL                 = 'M'
                              AND   MEME.MEME_CK                  = ELIG.MEME_CK 
                              AND   ELIG.MEPE_EFF_DT             <= '#CurrDate#' 
                              AND   ELIG.CSPD_CAT                        = 'M'
                              AND   ELIG.MEPE_ELIG_IND           = 'Y' 
                              AND    ELIG.SGSG_CK                    = SGSG.SGSG_CK 
                              AND
                                   ELIG.MEPE_TERM_DT = (SELECT MAX(ELIG2.MEPE_TERM_DT) 
                                                          FROM #$FacetsOwner#.CMC_MEPE_PRCS_ELIG  ELIG2
                                                         WHERE ELIG2.MEME_CK = ELIG.MEME_CK AND
                                                               ELIG2.MEPE_EFF_DT <= '#CurrDate#' AND
                                                               ELIG2.MEPE_ELIG_IND = 'Y' AND
                                                               ELIG2.CSPD_CAT = 'M')"

                        Target content:
                            extract_query_lnkSbsbMedCov = (
                                f"SELECT SBSB.SBSB_CK, SBSB.SBSB_ID, SGSG.SGSG_ID "
                                f"FROM {FacetsOwner}.CMC_BLEI_ENTY_INFO BLEI,"
                                f"     {FacetsOwner}.CMC_MEME_MEMBER MEME, "
                                f"     {FacetsOwner}.CMC_MEPE_PRCS_ELIG ELIG,"
                                f"     {FacetsOwner}.CMC_SBSB_SUBSC SBSB,"
                                f"     {FacetsOwner}.CMC_SGSG_SUB_GROUP SGSG "
                                f"WHERE BLEI.BLEI_BILL_LEVEL_CK = SBSB.SBSB_CK "
                                f"  AND BLEI.BLEI_BILL_LEVEL = 'I' "
                                f"  AND SBSB.SBSB_CK = MEME.SBSB_CK "
                                f"  AND MEME.MEME_REL = 'M' "
                                f"  AND MEME.MEME_CK = ELIG.MEME_CK "
                                f"  AND ELIG.MEPE_EFF_DT <= '#CurrDate#' "
                                f"  AND ELIG.CSPD_CAT = 'M' "
                                f"  AND ELIG.MEPE_ELIG_IND = 'Y' "
                                f"  AND ELIG.SGSG_CK = SGSG.SGSG_CK "
                                f"  AND ELIG.MEPE_TERM_DT = (SELECT MAX(ELIG2.MEPE_TERM_DT) "
                                f"                           FROM {FacetsOwner}.CMC_MEPE_PRCS_ELIG ELIG2 "
                                f"                           WHERE ELIG2.MEME_CK = ELIG.MEME_CK "
                                f"                             AND ELIG2.MEPE_EFF_DT <= '#CurrDate#' "
                                f"                             AND ELIG2.MEPE_ELIG_IND = 'Y' "
                                f"                             AND ELIG2.CSPD_CAT = 'M')"
                            )


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Replace every occurrence of the literal string "'#CurrDate#'" inside the SQL f-strings with the runtime variable value.
                           Example:
                             f"  AND ELIG.MEPE_EFF_DT <= '#CurrDate#' "
                           should become
                             f"  AND ELIG.MEPE_EFF_DT <= '{CurrDate}' "  # or use a proper parameter marker depending on the RDBMS.
                        
                        2. Do the same replacement for the sub-queries that compare MEPE_EFF_DT and MEPE_TERM_DT.
                        
                        3. Make this change consistently in all eight affected extract_query_* variables (lnkSbsbMedCov, lnkSbsbDentCov, lnkSbsbOthrCov, lnkSbsbMedNoCov, lnkSbsbDentNoCov, lnkSbsbOthrNoCov, plus any others that still contain the literal placeholder).
                        
                        4. If the target database supports bind parameters, prefer using the Spark JDBC `.option("query", query)` pattern with a parameter place-holder (`?`) and pass the CurrDate value via `jdbc_props_facets["currentDate"] = CurrDate` to avoid SQL-injection risks.
                        
                        5. Re-run the notebook to ensure the SQL now returns the same result set as the original DataStage job.

                    Check name:
                        SQL Query Translation

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage stage query contains a WHERE clause that restricts rows by the primary-key columns:

                        In the PySpark code, the JDBC query
                          SELECT BNF_VNDR_ID, BNF_SUM_DTL_TYP_CD, EFF_DT_SK, TERM_DT_SK, REMIT_RATE, USER_ID, LAST_UPDT_DT_SK FROM {UWSOwner}.BNF_VNDR_REMIT_RATE
                        has no WHERE clause, so all rows are read.  Therefore the filtering logic in the DataStage stage is not implemented.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            "SqlPrimary": "SELECT BNF_VNDR_ID, BNF_SUM_DTL_TYP_CD, EFF_DT_SK, TERM_DT_SK, REMIT_RATE, USER_ID, LAST_UPDT_DT_SK FROM #$UWSOwner#.BNF_VNDR_REMIT_RATE WHERE (BNF_VNDR_ID = ? AND BNF_SUM_DTL_TYP_CD = ? AND EFF_DT_SK = ?);"

                                .option(
                                    "query",
                                    "SELECT BNF_VNDR_ID, BNF_SUM_DTL_TYP_CD, EFF_DT_SK, TERM_DT_SK, REMIT_RATE, USER_ID, LAST_UPDT_DT_SK "
                                    f"FROM {UWSOwner}.BNF_VNDR_REMIT_RATE"
                                )
                                .load()
                            )


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Capture the three filter values as notebook parameters, e.g.
                           bnf_vndr_id  = get_widget_value('BNF_VNDR_ID')
                           bnf_sum_dtl  = get_widget_value('BNF_SUM_DTL_TYP_CD')

                           .option(
                               "query",

                        "

                           FORMAT.DATE(Strip.BLAC_CREATE_DTM, 'SYBASE', 'TIMESTMP', 'DB2TIMESTMP')
                        which yields a DB2-compatible timestamp string that includes fractional seconds (e.g. "YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS.ffffff").
                        
                        PySpark code instead uses
                           F.date_format(F.col("BLAC_CREATE_DTM"), "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss")
                        which drops the fractional-second component and therefore does not match the required format.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            {"ColumnName": "BLAC_CREATE_DTM", "PrimaryKey": "true", "Expression": "FORMAT.DATE(Strip.BLAC_CREATE_DTM, 'SYBASE', 'TIMESTMP', 'DB2TIMESTMP')"}

                        Target content:

                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Replace the current transformation for BLAC_CREATE_DTM in df_allcol.
                        2. Re-create the DB2 timestamp layout exactly, including the milliseconds/microseconds part.  A direct equivalent to the DataStage logic can be built the same way CRT_DTM is built for the Transform link:
                        
                        .withColumn(
                            "BLAC_CREATE_DTM",
                            F.concat(
                                F.substring(F.col("BLAC_CREATE_DTM"),1,10), F.lit(" "),
                                F.substring(F.col("BLAC_CREATE_DTM"),12,2), F.lit(":"),
                                F.substring(F.col("BLAC_CREATE_DTM"),15,2), F.lit(":"),

--- Item 330 ---
    All stages passed:
        False

    Failed checks:
        --- Item 1 ---
            Passed all checks:
                False

            Failed checks:
                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        1

                    Check name:
                        SQL Query Translation

                    Explanation:
                        In the DataStage stage, the table #$IDSOwner#.W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST is required for the SELECT query on the Output pin and therefore must still exist when that query is executed.  The PySpark code, however, executes
                        
                            execute_dml(f"DROP TABLE {IDSOwner}.W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST", ...)
                        
                        BEFORE running the SELECT (extract_query_ibrh) that reads from the very same table.  Dropping the table first breaks the logical order of operations defined in DataStage and will cause the subsequent SELECT to fail, so the SQL has not been faithfully translated.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            INSERT INTO #$IDSOwner#.W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST (SRC_SYS_CD_SK,CLM_ID) VALUES (ORCHESTRATE.SRC_SYS_CD_SK,ORCHESTRATE.CLM_ID)
                            
                            CREATE TABLE #$IDSOwner#.W_PAYMT_SUM (
                            SRC_SYS_CD_SK INTEGER NOT NULL, 
                            PAYMT_REF_ID CHAR(16) NOT NULL, 
                            PAYMT_SUM_LOB_CD CHAR(4) NOT NULL
                             )
                            
                            DROP TABLE #$IDSOwner#.W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST

                        Target content:
                            # CREATE TABLE #$IDSOwner#.W_PAYMT_SUM
                            execute_dml(
                                f"""
                                CREATE TABLE {IDSOwner}.W_PAYMT_SUM (
                                   SRC_SYS_CD_SK INTEGER NOT NULL,
                                   PAYMT_REF_ID CHAR(16) NOT NULL,
                                   PAYMT_SUM_LOB_CD CHAR(4) NOT NULL
                                )
                                """,
                                jdbc_url,
                                jdbc_props
                            )
                            
                            # DROP TABLE #$IDSOwner#.W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST
                            execute_dml(
                                f"DROP TABLE {IDSOwner}.W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST",
                                jdbc_url,
                                jdbc_props
                            )
                            
                            # AfterSql => RUNSTATS
                            execute_dml(
                                f"CALL SYSPROC.ADMIN_CMD('runstats on table {IDSOwner}.W_PAYMT_SUM on key columns with distribution on key columns and detailed indexes all allow write access')",
                                jdbc_url,
                                jdbc_props
                            )
                            
                            # 4) OutputPin => "Keys" => we do a DB read with the query
                            extract_query_ibrh = f"""
                            SELECT 
                                  k.SRC_SYS_CD_SK,
                                  k.CLM_ID,
                                  k.CLM_SK
                            FROM 
                                  {IDSOwner}.W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST w,
                                  {IDSOwner}.K_CLM k
                            WHERE 
                                  w.SRC_SYS_CD_SK = k.SRC_SYS_CD_SK
                              AND w.CLM_ID = k.CLM_ID
                            """


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Remove or defer the `DROP TABLE {IDSOwner}.W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST` statement until AFTER the `extract_query_ibrh` SELECT has executed and the data has been read into Spark.
                        2. A simple way is to move the DROP (and optionally the RUNSTATS) to the end of the stage logic, right after the `spark.read.format("jdbc")...load()` operation finishes.
                        3. Ensure that the order of operations matches the DataStage semantics:
                           a. TRUNCATE W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST
                           b. INSERT (write) data into W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST
                           c. CREATE W_PAYMT_SUM
                           d. READ the SELECT query for the Output pin
                           e. DROP W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST (if still required)
                           f. CALL RUNSTATS on W_PAYMT_SUM
                        4. Re-run unit tests to verify the SELECT now succeeds and no table-not-found errors occur.



            Stage index:
                3

            Stage name:
                W_BILL_RDUCTN_HIST

            Stage type:

                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        0

                    Check name:
                        Parameters Check - sort_cols

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage stage specifies the keys that must drive the sort within each duplicate-partition:
                          • Filter.RetainRecord = "first"
                          • InputPins[0].KeySortOrder = "INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK, ACCTG_DT_SK, SRC_SYS_CD"
                        
                        Therefore sort_cols has to be
                          [('INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK','A'), ('ACCTG_DT_SK','A'), ('SRC_SYS_CD','A')]
                        
                        In the PySpark implementation the call to dedup_sort passes an empty list instead:
                          sort_cols=[]
                        This omits the required ordering columns, so the PySpark logic does not faithfully replicate the DataStage behaviour.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            "Filter": {"RetainRecord": "first", "KeysThatDefineDuplicates": "INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK, ACCTG_DT_SK, SRC_SYS_CD"},
                            "InputPins": [{
                              "SortMode": "-nonStable", 
                              "KeySortOrder": "INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK, ACCTG_DT_SK, SRC_SYS_CD"
                            }]

                        Target content:
                            df_rdp_NaturalKeys = dedup_sort(
                                df_lnkRemDupDataIn,
                                partition_cols=["INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK","ACCTG_DT_SK","SRC_SYS_CD"],
                                sort_cols=[]
                            )


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Derive the sort columns from the KeySortOrder list in the DataStage JSON.
                        2. Because RetainRecord is "first" and no explicit order is supplied, use Ascending order for each column.
                        3. Replace the empty sort_cols argument with the correct list of tuples:
                        
                        ```
                        sort_cols=[
                            ("INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK", "A"),
                            ("ACCTG_DT_SK", "A"),
                            ("SRC_SYS_CD", "A")
                        ]
                        ```
                        4. The corrected call becomes:
                        
                        ```
                        df_rdp_NaturalKeys = dedup_sort(
                            df_lnkRemDupDataIn,
                            partition_cols=["INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK", "ACCTG_DT_SK", "SRC_SYS_CD"],
                            sort_cols=[
                                ("INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK", "A"),
                                ("ACCTG_DT_SK", "A"),
                                ("SRC_SYS_CD", "A")
                            ]
                        )
                        ```
                        5. If dedup_sort already assumes ascending order when only column names are provided, you can alternatively pass a simple list of column strings, but it must not be left empty.

        --- Item 3 ---
            Passed all checks:

            Failed checks:
                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        2

                    Check name:
                        SQL Query Translation

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage stage "db2_K_InterPlnBill_Load" is configured with:
                        • Connection_WriteMode = "Insert"
                        • TableAction = "Append"
                        meaning it should only append new rows to the table #$IDSOwner#.K_INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS.
                        
                        However, the PySpark code issues a MERGE statement that both INSERTS and UPDATES existing rows (see the WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE clause). Adding UPDATE logic changes business behaviour and is not equivalent to a pure INSERT-APPEND operation required by the DataStage stage.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            {"StageName": "db2_K_InterPlnBill_Load", "StageType": "DB2ConnectorPX", "Database": "IDS", "SQL": "#$IDSOwner#.K_INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS", "Connection_WriteMode": "Insert", "TableAction": "Append", "InputPins": [{"LinkIdentifier": "V72S3P1", "LinkName": "lnk_KAccum_new", "InputIdentifiers": "V72S2|V72S2P2", "InputStageName": "xfm_PKEYgen", "SortMode": "-nonStable"}]}

                        Target content:
                            merge_sql_db2_K_InterPlnBill_Load = f"""
                            MERGE INTO {IDSOwner}.K_INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS AS target
                            USING {temp_table_db2_K_InterPlnBill_Load} AS source
                            ON
                            (
                              target.INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK = source.INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK
                              AND target.ACCTG_DT_SK = source.ACCTG_DT_SK
                              AND target.SRC_SYS_CD = source.SRC_SYS_CD
                            )
                            WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET
                              target.CRT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK = source.CRT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK,
                              target.INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_SK = source.INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_SK
                            WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN
                              INSERT
                              (
                                INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK,
                                ACCTG_DT_SK,
                                SRC_SYS_CD,
                                CRT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK,
                                INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_SK
                              )
                              VALUES
                              (
                                source.INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_CK,
                                source.ACCTG_DT_SK,
                                source.SRC_SYS_CD,
                                source.CRT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK,
                                source.INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS_SK
                              );
                            """


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Remove the MERGE statement and its execution.
                        2. Replace it with a straight INSERT (append) using either:
                           a) spark-based JDBC write:
                              df_lnk_KAccum_new.write \
                                  .format("jdbc") \
                                  .option("url", jdbc_url_db2_K_InterPlnBill_Load) \
                                  .options(**jdbc_props_db2_K_InterPlnBill_Load) \
                                  .option("dbtable", f"{IDSOwner}.K_INTER_PLN_BILL_TRANS") \
                                  .mode("append") \
                                  .save()
                           or
                           b) An explicit INSERT INTO … SELECT … SQL statement executed through execute_dml(), but without any UPDATE clause.
                        3. Ensure no additional UPDATE logic is introduced so behaviour matches the original DataStage "Insert/Append" semantics.
                        4. Keep semicolon off the plain INSERT statement (allowed only for MERGE).

                        For IDS (an Azure SQL target) the DB2 function SUBSTR must be translated to the T-SQL equivalent SUBSTRING. 
                        
                        DataStage SQL uses:
                          SUBSTR(A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,5,4) AS FINALLOB
                        
                        The PySpark script keeps the DB2 syntax unchanged inside the query string `extract_query_ids`, so it will fail when executed against Azure SQL.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            "SQL": "SELECT A.LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD  AS LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                            \tA.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD AS GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                            \tSUBSTR(A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,5,4) AS FINALLOB
                            FROM #$IDSOwner#.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK A,\t
                            (SELECT SRC_SYS_CD,
                            \tLGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD,\t\t
                            \tMAX(JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK_SK) AS MXSK,
                            \tMAX(LAST_UPDT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK) AS MXUPDTSK
                            FROM #$IDSOwner#.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK
                            WHERE FNCL_LOB_CD NOT IN ('UNK', 'NA')
                            GROUP BY SRC_SYS_CD,
                            \tLGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD) MX
                            
                            WHERE A.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK_SK = MX.MXSK
                            AND A.LAST_UPDT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK = MX.MXUPDTSK"

                        Target content:
                            extract_query_ids = f"""SELECT A.LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD  AS LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                                   A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD AS GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                                   SUBSTR(A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,5,4) AS FINALLOB
                              FROM {IDSOwner}.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK A,
                                   (
                                     SELECT SRC_SYS_CD,
                                            LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                                            MAX(JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK_SK) AS MXSK,
                                            MAX(LAST_UPDT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK) AS MXUPDTSK
                                       FROM {IDSOwner}.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK
                                      WHERE FNCL_LOB_CD NOT IN ('UNK', 'NA')
                                      GROUP BY SRC_SYS_CD,
                                               LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD
                                   ) MX
                             WHERE A.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK_SK = MX.MXSK
                               AND A.LAST_UPDT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK = MX.MXUPDTSK
                            """


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Replace the DB2 `SUBSTR` function with the T-SQL `SUBSTRING` function.
                           Example:
                              SUBSTRING(A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD, 5, 4) AS FINALLOB
                        2. Ensure the argument order remains `(expression, start, length)` – identical to DB2 – so no other changes are needed.
                        3. Update the `extract_query_ids` string in the PySpark script accordingly:
                        ```
                        extract_query_ids = f"""SELECT A.LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD  AS LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                               A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD AS GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                               SUBSTRING(A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD, 5, 4) AS FINALLOB
                        ... (rest of query remains unchanged) ...
                        """
                        ```
                        4. Validate the revised query against Azure SQL to confirm successful execution.

                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        0

                    Check name:
                        Funnel Union Implementation

                    Explanation:
                        DataStage stage "fnl_NA_UNK_Streams" is a PxFunnel, therefore the three input streams (Lnk_Main, Lnk_UNK, Lnk_NA) must be combined with unionByName so that columns are matched by name.  
                        
                        In the PySpark code the streams are combined with the plain union() method:
                        ```
                        df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_union = df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_main.union(df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_unk).union(df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_na)
                        ```
                        union() relies on positional order and disregards column names, which violates the requirement.  
                        
                        Because of this, the logic of the DataStage Funnel is not faithfully reproduced.  In addition, the column list in the third input uses the miss-spelled column name "FNcl_LOB_SK" which will further break the alignment.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            {"StageName": "fnl_NA_UNK_Streams", "StageType": "PxFunnel", "InputPins": [{"LinkIdentifier": "V86S4P1", "LinkName": "Lnk_Main", "InputIdentifiers": "V86S6|V86S6P2", "InputStageName": "xfm_CheckLkpResults"}, {"LinkIdentifier": "V86S4P2", "LinkName": "Lnk_UNK", "InputIdentifiers": "V86S6|V86S6P4", "InputStageName": "xfm_CheckLkpResults"}, {"LinkIdentifier": "V86S4P3", "LinkName": "Lnk_NA", "InputIdentifiers": "V86S6|V86S6P5", "InputStageName": "xfm_CheckLkpResults"}], "OutputPins": [{"LinkIdentifier": "V86S4P4", "LinkName": "Lnk_IdsONEXCHFkey_EE_OutABC", ...}], "Parameters": [{"Name": "operator", "Value": "funnel"}]}

                        Target content:
                            df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_union = df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_main.union(df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_unk).union(df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_na)


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Replace every use of union() that combines the three Funnel input dataframes with unionByName():
                           ```python
                           df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_union = (
                               df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_main
                                 .unionByName(df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_unk)
                                 .unionByName(df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_na)
                           )
                           ```
                        2. Make sure the schemas of the three input dataframes are identical before the unionByName.  In particular correct the typo in the NA stream selection:
                           ```python
                           df_fnl_NA_UNK_Streams_na = df_xfm_Lnk_NA.select(
                               "ON_EXCH_FED_PAYMT_TRANS_SK",
                               "ON_EXCH_FED_PAYMT_TRANS_CK",
                               "ACCTG_DT_SK",
                               "SOURCE_SYSTEM_CODE_SK",
                               "CRT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK",
                               "LAST_UPDT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK",
                               "FNCL_LOB_SK",        # <-- fix spelling
                               "GRP_SK",
                               "PROD_SK",
                               "RCVD_INCM_SBSDY_DTL_PAYMT_TYP_CD_SK",
                               "COV_STRT_DT_SK",
                               "COV_END_DT_SK",
                               "PROD_LOB_NO",
                               "EXCH_MBR_ID",
                               "SUB_ID"
                           )
                           ```
                        3. Re-run the notebook to ensure the Funnel output matches the DataStage specification.

                    Check name:
                        Funnel/Union implementation

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage "FnlFkeyFailures" funnel stage must append the three input streams (lnk_GrpTypCdLkup_Fail, lnk_ProdTypCdLkup_Fail, lnk_FnclLobTypCdLkup_Fail) using a union-by-column-name strategy (per the translation rule: "should be translated to a series of PySpark `.unionByName()` calls").  
                        
                        In the PySpark code the union is performed with the positional `.union()` method:
                        ```
                        df_FnlFkeyFailures_union = df_fnlFkeyFailures_Grp.union(df_fnlFkeyFailures_Prod).union(df_fnlFkeyFailures_FnclLob)
                        ```
                        `.union()` aligns columns by position, not by name; therefore it does not guarantee that each column (e.g., PRI_SK, PRI_NAT_KEY_STRING, SRC_SYS_CD_SK …) is mapped correctly if the upstream schema order ever differs.  The required `.unionByName()` call is missing.

                --- Item 1 ---

                    Check name:
                        sort_cols Parameter Verification

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage stage specifies sorting on the keys "ON_EXCH_FED_PAYMT_TRANS_CK, ACCTG_DT_SK, SRC_SYS_CD" (InputPins[0].KeySortOrder) and with RetainRecord="first" an ascending sort should be applied.                     Failed check index:
                        2

                    Check name:
                        SQL Query Translation

                    Explanation:
                        The SQL in the PySpark script keeps the DB2-specific function "SUBSTR".  For Azure SQL (used through the IDS JDBC connection obtained via get_db_config(ids_secret_name)), the correct function name is "SUBSTRING".  Because the rule for IDS/EDW/BCBS databases requires DB2 SQL to be translated to Azure-SQL syntax, leaving "SUBSTR" unconverted violates the translation requirement.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            "SQL": "SELECT A.LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD  AS LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                            \tA.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD AS GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                            \tSUBSTR(A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,5,4) AS FINALLOB
                            FROM #$IDSOwner#.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK A,\t
                            (SELECT SRC_SYS_CD,
                            \tLGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD,\t\t
                            \tMAX(JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK_SK) AS MXSK,
                            \tMAX(LAST_UPDT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK) AS MXUPDTSK
                            FROM #$IDSOwner#.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK
                            WHERE FNCL_LOB_CD NOT IN ('UNK', 'NA')
                            GROUP BY SRC_SYS_CD,
                            \tLGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD) MX
                            
                            WHERE A.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK_SK = MX.MXSK
                            AND A.LAST_UPDT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK = MX.MXUPDTSK"

                        Target content:
                            f"""
                            SELECT A.LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD  AS LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                                   A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD AS GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                                   SUBSTR(A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD,5,4) AS FINALLOB
                              FROM {IDSOwner}.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK A,
                                   (
                                     SELECT SRC_SYS_CD,
                                            LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD,
                                            MAX(JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK_SK) AS MXSK,
                                            MAX(LAST_UPDT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK) AS MXUPDTSK
                                       FROM {IDSOwner}.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK
                                      WHERE FNCL_LOB_CD NOT IN ('UNK', 'NA')
                                      GROUP BY SRC_SYS_CD,
                                               LGCY_FNCL_LOB_CD
                                   ) MX
                             WHERE A.JRNL_ENTRY_TRANS_CRSWALK_SK = MX.MXSK
                               AND A.LAST_UPDT_RUN_CYC_EXCTN_SK = MX.MXUPDTSK
                            """


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Replace the DB2 function SUBSTR with the Azure-SQL equivalent SUBSTRING.
                           Example:
                           SUBSTRING(A.GL_FNCL_LOB_CD, 5, 4) AS FINALLOB
                        2. Keep all other parts of the query unchanged, retaining {IDSOwner} parameterisation.
                        3. Ensure no semicolon is placed at the end of the SELECT statement.
                        4. Re-run the notebook to confirm the query executes successfully against the IDS Azure SQL database.

        --- Item 1 ---

                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        4

                    Check name:
                        Output Column Expression Check

                    Explanation:
                        DataStage wants: If LEN(JeCrrntLob.FINALLOB)=0 then TRIM(GL_DTL_LOB) else TRIM(FINALLOB).  In the PySpark code the condition uses F.length(F.col("FINALLOB"))==0, but FINALLOB has already been padded with four spaces (rpad) earlier, so its length is always 4, never 0.  Therefore rows whose FINALLOB is blank will take the ELSE branch, producing an incorrect value.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            {"ColumnName": "FINALLOB", "SqlType": "char", "Length": "4", "Expression": " If LEN(JeCrrntLob.FINALLOB) = 0 Then TRIM(JeCrrntLob.GL_DTL_LOB) Else TRIM(JeCrrntLob.FINALLOB)"}

                        Target content:
                            F.rpad(
                                F.when(F.length(F.col("FINALLOB")) == 0, trim(F.col("GL_DTL_LOB"))).otherwise(trim(F.col("FINALLOB"))),
                                4, " "
                            ).alias("FINALLOB")


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Remove the prior rpad on IdsLobCds.FINALLOB when creating df_JeCrrntLob, or trim it before the length test.
                        2. Change the condition to test the trimmed value:  
                           F.when(F.length(F.trim(F.col("FINALLOB"))) == 0, trim(F.col("GL_DTL_LOB")))  
                           or  
                           F.when(F.trim(F.col("FINALLOB")) == "", trim(F.col("GL_DTL_LOB"))).
                        3. Keep the rpad(…,4," ") outside the WHEN/OTHERWISE logic as it is now.
                        4. Re-run the script and verify that rows with blank FINALLOB now correctly pick GL_DTL_LOB.

                           final_file_path = f"{adls_path_publish}/external/{Environment}_JRNL_ENTRY_{Trans_JeNme}_TOTALS.txt"

                        SQL Query Translation

                    Explanation:
                        The SQL in the DataStage stage references the database parameter "#$BCBSFINDB#" (e.g., "FROM #$BCBSFINDB#..GL_COM_DTL_BCP").
                        
                        In the PySpark script the query string uses the variable "BCBSFINOwner" instead ("FROM {BCBSFINOwner}..GL_COM_DTL_BCP").  This substitutes the schema/owner value for the database name, altering the 3-part identifier and therefore does not faithfully reproduce the original SQL.
                        
                        DataStage: database..table (schema left blank)
                        PySpark :  schema..table (database left blank)
                        
                        Consequently the query might address a different object or fail at runtime, so the translation of the SQL is incorrect.

                    Failure snippet:

                    Explanation:
                        In the DataStage definition the column LINE_DESC is NOT marked as a PrimaryKey (grouping key).

                        PySpark implementation additionally places LINE_DESC inside the groupBy clause, which makes it an extra grouping key and changes the level of aggregation:
                        ```.groupBy(
                            "BUSINESS_UNIT",
                            "TRANSACTION_LINE",
                            "ACCOUNTING_DT",
                            "BUSINESS_UNIT_GL",
                            "ACCOUNT",
                            "DEPTID",

                        PySpark expression contains an extra WHEN clause:

                            F.when((F.col("BUSINESS_UNIT_GL").isin("0","NA")), F.lit(" ")).otherwise(F.col("BUSINESS_UNIT_GL"))

                    Check name:

                    Explanation:
                        For several columns whose DataStage expressions include TRIM(<column>), the PySpark code applies only rpad() without trimming.  Specifically:
                         • APP_JRNL_ID – DataStage: TRIM(lnkJrnlSumFieldsToTrns.APP_JRNL_ID); PySpark: F.rpad(F.col("APP_JRNL_ID"), 10, " ")
                         • ACCOUNT – DataStage: TRIM(lnkJrnlSumFieldsToTrns.ACCOUNT); PySpark: F.rpad(F.col("ACCOUNT"), 10, " ")
                         • DEPTID – DataStage: TRIM(lnkJrnlSumFieldsToTrns.DEPTID); PySpark: F.rpad(F.col("DEPTID"), 10, " ")
                         • AFFILIATE – DataStage: TRIM(lnkJrnlSumFieldsToTrns.AFFILIATE); PySpark: F.rpad(F.col("AFFILIATE"), 5, " ")
                         • CHARTFIELD1 – DataStage: TRIM(lnkJrnlSumFieldsToTrns.CHARTFIELD1); PySpark: F.rpad(F.col("CHARTFIELD1"), 10, " ")
                        The absence of F.trim means trailing and leading spaces remain, violating the DataStage logic.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:

                        Target content:

                                F.rpad(F.col("APP_JRNL_ID"), 10, " ").alias("APP_JRNL_ID"),
                                F.rpad(F.col("BUSINESS_UNIT"), 5, " ").alias("BUSINESS_UNIT"),
                                F.rpad(F.col("ACCOUNT"), 10, " ").alias("ACCOUNT"),

                                F.rpad(F.col("AFFILIATE"), 5, " ").alias("AFFILIATE"),

                                ... )

                        Trim the relevant columns before applying rpad:
                        
                        ```
                        df_trns_hdr_fields = df_agg.filter(F.col("MONETARY_AMOUNT") != 0.00).select(
                            F.rpad(F.trim(F.col("APP_JRNL_ID")), 10, " ").alias("APP_JRNL_ID"),
                            F.rpad(F.col("BUSINESS_UNIT"), 5, " ").alias("BUSINESS_UNIT"),

                            F.rpad(F.trim(F.col("DEPTID")), 10, " ").alias("DEPTID"),
                            F.rpad(F.trim(F.col("AFFILIATE")), 5, " ").alias("AFFILIATE"),
                            "MONETARY_AMOUNT",
                            F.rpad(F.trim(F.col("CHARTFIELD1")), 10, " ").alias("CHARTFIELD1"),
                            F.rpad(F.col("LINE_DESC"), 30, " ").alias("LINE_DESC"),
                            F.rpad(F.col("GL_FNCL_LOB_CD"), 20, " ").alias("GL_FNCL_LOB_CD"),
                            F.rpad(F.col("BANK_ACCT_NM"), 100, " ").alias("BANK_ACCT_NM"),
                            F.rpad(F.col("RVNU_CAT_ID"), 20, " ").alias("RVNU_CAT_ID"),
                            F.rpad(F.col("SPEND_CAT_ID"), 20, " ").alias("SPEND_CAT_ID"),
                            F.rpad(F.col("JRNL_LN_REF_NO"), 20, " ").alias("JRNL_LN_REF_NO")
                        )
                        ```
                        This applies the required trimming while keeping the right-padding for char fixed-length fields.

                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        2

                    Check name:
                        SQL Query Translation

                    Explanation:
                        In the DataStage stage the FROM-clause points to the database placeholder "#$BCBSFINDB#" (the BCBS Finance database):
                        
                          FROM #$BCBSFINDB#..GL_#TableInterface#_DTL_BCP GL_BCP
                        
                        In the PySpark translation this placeholder was replaced with the unrelated widget variable "FilePath":
                        
                          FROM {FilePath.replace('#','')}..GL_{TableInterface}_DTL_BCP GL_BCP
                        
                        As a result the query is executed against whatever value happens to be in FilePath instead of the BCBS database.  This breaks the logical equivalence of the stage.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            SELECT 
                            GL_BCP.GL_#ColumnName#_DTL_CK as GL_DTL_CK, 
                            GL_BCP.ACCOUNT, 
                            GL_BCP.OPERATING_UNIT, 
                            GL_BCP.AFFILIATE, 
                            GL_BCP.DEPTID, 
                            GL_BCP.CHARTFIELD1,
                            GL_BCP.JRNL_LN_REF
                            
                             FROM #$BCBSFINDB#..GL_#TableInterface#_DTL_BCP GL_BCP;

                        Target content:
                            extract_query_BCP = (
                                f"SELECT 
                            "
                                f"GL_BCP.GL_{ColumnName}_DTL_CK as GL_DTL_CK, 
                            "
                                f"GL_BCP.ACCOUNT, 
                            "
                                f"GL_BCP.OPERATING_UNIT, 
                            "
                                f"GL_BCP.AFFILIATE, 
                            "
                                f"GL_BCP.DEPTID, 
                            "
                                f"GL_BCP.CHARTFIELD1,
                            "
                                f"GL_BCP.JRNL_LN_REF
                            "
                                f"FROM {FilePath.replace('#','')}..GL_{TableInterface}_DTL_BCP GL_BCP"
                            )


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Introduce a widget (or hard-coded constant) that captures the BCBS Finance database name, e.g.
                              bcbs_fin_db = get_widget_value('BCBSFINDB','')
                           or if the name is fixed just use the literal database name.
                        
                        2. Replace the FROM-clause construction with the correct database placeholder:
                              f"FROM {bcbs_fin_db}..GL_{TableInterface}_DTL_BCP GL_BCP"
                        
                        3. Remove any reference to FilePath from this query; FilePath is used for file locations, not database names.
                        
                        4. Verify that the same variable (bcbs_fin_db) is also used in any other BCBS SQL in the notebook to ensure consistency.
                        
                        5. Keep the semicolon off the end of the query (current implementation already satisfies this rule).

                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        2

                    Check name:
                        SQL Query Translation

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage stage requests only the rows that satisfy the predicates "K_CLM.SRC_SYS_CD_SK=? AND K_CLM.CLM_ID=?".  
                        However, the PySpark code loads the whole table:
                        ```
                        .option("query", f"SELECT K_CLM.SRC_SYS_CD_SK, K_CLM.CLM_ID, K_CLM.CLM_SK FROM {IDSOwner}.K_CLM K_CLM")
                        ```
                        Because the WHERE clause is missing, the logic is not equivalent to the DataStage SQL and the entire table is transferred from the database.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            {"StageName": "K_CLM", "StageType": "DB2Connector", "Database": "IDS", "SQL": "SELECT K_CLM.SRC_SYS_CD_SK as SRC_SYS_CD_SK,K_CLM.CLM_ID as CLM_ID,K_CLM.CLM_SK as CLM_SK FROM #$IDSOwner#.K_CLM K_CLM WHERE K_CLM.SRC_SYS_CD_SK=? AND K_CLM.CLM_ID=?
                            
                            #$IDSOwner#.K_CLM K_CLM", "Connection_WriteMode": "Insert", "TableAction": "Append", "OutputPins": [{"LinkIdentifier": "V0S81P1", "LinkName": "clm_lookup", "OutputIdentifiers": "V0S15|V0S15P9", "OutputStageName": "BusinessRules"}]}

                        Target content:
                            jdbc_url_ids, jdbc_props_ids = get_db_config(ids_secret_name)
                            df_K_CLM = (
                                spark.read.format("jdbc")
                                .option("url", jdbc_url_ids)
                                .options(**jdbc_props_ids)
                                .option("query", f"SELECT K_CLM.SRC_SYS_CD_SK, K_CLM.CLM_ID, K_CLM.CLM_SK FROM {IDSOwner}.K_CLM K_CLM")
                                .load()
                            )


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Include the required WHERE clause in the SQL passed to the JDBC reader so that only the needed records are retrieved.
                        
                        Example:
                        ```
                        query_K_CLM = (
                            f"SELECT K_CLM.SRC_SYS_CD_SK, K_CLM.CLM_ID, K_CLM.CLM_SK "
                            f"FROM {IDSOwner}.K_CLM K_CLM "
                            f"WHERE K_CLM.SRC_SYS_CD_SK = {SrcSysCdSk} "
                            f"AND K_CLM.CLM_ID = '{clm_id_var}'"   # clm_id_var should carry the CLM_ID value
                        )
                        
                        df_K_CLM = (
                            spark.read.format("jdbc")
                            .option("url", jdbc_url_ids)
                            .options(**jdbc_props_ids)
                            .option("query", query_K_CLM)
                            .load()
                        )
                        ```
                        2. If several CLM_ID values are required, create a list of the distinct CLM_IDs from the driving DataFrame and use it to build an "IN (...)" clause, or push the filtering after the load with `df_K_CLM.filter(...)`, but make sure the end-result matches the predicate `SRC_SYS_CD_SK=? AND CLM_ID=?` for every lookup request.
                        3. Verify that no semicolon is appended to the query string (per style rule).

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage stage selects 65 columns, including the trailing set of Workday columns (WD_LOB_CD, WD_BANK_ACCT_NM, WD_RVNU_CAT_ID, WD_SPEND_CAT_ID, WD_CC_ID, WD_DNR_ID, WD_AFFIL_ID, WD_CUST_ID, WD_ACCT_NO).  
                        The PySpark translation reads the same table but the SQL query ends at GL_DISTRIB_STATUS and omits the final 10 WD_* columns.  
                        Because these columns are later referenced in the code (e.g.  `col("BCPExtract.WD_ACCT_NO")`, `col("BCPExtract.WD_AFFIL_ID")`), the query is incomplete and does not faithfully replicate the DataStage logic.

--- Item 375 ---

    Failed checks:
        --- Item 1 ---
            Passed all checks:
                False

            Failed checks:
                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        3

                    Check name:
                        Output Pin Constraint Implementation

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage constraint for the "Notify" output pin is:
                        @INROWNUM = 1 And ToleranceCd = 'OUT'
                        This means a row must be written ONLY when (a) the parameter ToleranceCd equals the literal string 'OUT' AND (b) the current input row is the very first row processed by the stage (INROWNUM = 1).
                        
                        In the PySpark script, however, the logic is implemented as:
                        ```
                        if ToleranceCd == 'OUT':
                            df_TransformLogic_Notify = spark.createDataFrame(
                                [("ROW COUNT BALANCING PSI - IDS JRNL ENTRY TRANS INCM OUT OF TOLERANCE",)],
                                StructType([StructField("NOTIFICATION", StringType(), True)])
                            )
                        else:
                            df_TransformLogic_Notify = spark.createDataFrame(
                                [],
                                StructType([StructField("NOTIFICATION", StringType(), True)])
                            )
                        ```
                        This code completely ignores the INROWNUM = 1 condition. It always emits one notification row whenever the parameter `ToleranceCd` equals 'OUT', even if the input DataFrame `df_SrcTrgtComp` is empty. In the original DataStage logic, no output row would be produced when the input has zero rows because there would be no row with INROWNUM = 1.
                        

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            {"LinkIdentifier": "V0S19P4", "LinkName": "Notify", "OutputIdentifiers": "V0S22|V0S22P1", "OutputStageName": "ErrorNotificationFile", "Constraint": "@INROWNUM = 1 And ToleranceCd = 'OUT'", "Columns": [{"ColumnName": "NOTIFICATION", "SqlType": "char", "Length": "70", "WhereExpression": "\"ROW COUNT BALANCING PSI - IDS JRNL ENTRY TRANS INCM OUT OF TOLERANCE\""}]}

                        Target content:
                            # ------------------------------------------------------------------
                            if ToleranceCd == 'OUT':
                                df_TransformLogic_Notify = spark.createDataFrame(
                                    [("ROW COUNT BALANCING PSI - IDS JRNL ENTRY TRANS INCM OUT OF TOLERANCE",)],
                                    StructType([StructField("NOTIFICATION", StringType(), True)])
                                )
                            else:
                                df_TransformLogic_Notify = spark.createDataFrame(
                                    [],
                                    StructType([StructField("NOTIFICATION", StringType(), True)])
                                )
                            
                            # Apply rpad to 70 characters
                            df_TransformLogic_Notify = df_TransformLogic_Notify.select(
                                rpad(col("NOTIFICATION"), 70, " ").alias("NOTIFICATION")
                            )
                            


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Start with the full input DataFrame (`df_SrcTrgtComp`) because INROWNUM is evaluated over the rows coming from the input link "Missing".
                        2. Add a column that represents the row number in the original order.  The most direct way to mimic INROWNUM is to use `F.monotonically_increasing_id()` or `F.row_number()` over a dummy window.
                           ```python
                           from pyspark.sql import functions as F, Window
                        
                           w = Window.orderBy(F.lit(1))  # any constant ordering; INROWNUM just counts rows as they are read
                           df_with_rownum = df_SrcTrgtComp.withColumn('INROWNUM', F.row_number().over(w))
                           ```
                        3. Filter the DataFrame with both conditions exactly as in the DataStage constraint:
                           ```python
                           df_TransformLogic_Notify = df_with_rownum.filter( (col('INROWNUM') == 1) & (ToleranceCd == 'OUT') )
                           ```
                           Note: `ToleranceCd` is a Python variable, so embed it as a literal:
                           ```python
                           df_TransformLogic_Notify = df_with_rownum.filter( (col('INROWNUM') == 1) & (F.lit(ToleranceCd) == 'OUT') )
                           ```
                        4. After filtering, project the single constant column using `F.lit()` and apply `rpad()` so the output column matches the char(70) spec:
                           ```python
                           df_TransformLogic_Notify = df_TransformLogic_Notify.select(
                               rpad(F.lit("ROW COUNT BALANCING PSI - IDS JRNL ENTRY TRANS INCM OUT OF TOLERANCE"), 70, " ").alias("NOTIFICATION")
                           )
                           ```
                        5. Keep the remainder of the file-write logic unchanged.
                        
                        With these steps, the PySpark implementation will only output one notification row when BOTH conditions (`ToleranceCd == 'OUT'` and `INROWNUM == 1`) are satisfied, perfectly aligning with the original DataStage stage behavior.

                            )

                        1. Import pyspark.sql.functions as F (already present).
                        2. Replace current select with logic that first removes carriage return, line feed and tab characters:

                            trim(F.regexp_replace(F.col("CRFD_FUND_ID"), "[\r
                        \t]", "")).alias("CRFD_FUND_ID"),

                        \t]", "")).alias("CRFD_ACCT_CAT")
                        )
                        ```

        --- Item 3 ---

            Failed checks:
                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        1

                    Check name:
                        Input DataFrames Implementation & Collector Logic

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage CCollector stage "Collector_Paid_Adjs" specifies COLLECTALG = "Round-Robin", meaning the rows from the two inputs (in1 and DSLink103) must be inter-leaved alternately in the output.  
                        
                        In the PySpark implementation the two input DataFrames are simply concatenated with unionByName, which preserves all rows but does **not** guarantee round-robin ordering; it places all rows from the first DataFrame followed by all rows from the second.  Therefore the stage logic is not faithfully reproduced.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            "StageType": "CCollector",
                            "StageName": "Collector_Paid_Adjs",
                            "InputPins": [
                              { "LinkName": "in1",        "InputStageName": "hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_in1" },
                              { "LinkName": "DSLink103",   "InputStageName": "hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_in2" }
                            ],
                            "OutputPins": [ { "LinkName": "link_out", "OutputStageName": "hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_driver" } ],
                            "Parameters": [ { "Name": "COLLECTALG", "Value": "Round-Robin" } ]

                        Target content:
                            df_collector_in1 = df_hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_in1.select(df_hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_in1.columns)
                            df_collector_in2 = df_hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_in2.select(df_hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_in2.columns)
                            # Round-robin: we will just union them
                            df_Collector_Paid_Adjs = df_collector_in1.unionByName(df_collector_in2)


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Add a monotonically increasing id (row number) to each input DataFrame.
                        2. Use modulo arithmetic to inter-leave the two sets of rows so that the final ordering alternates between the sources.
                           Example:
                           ```python
                           from pyspark.sql import functions as F, Window
                        
                           def add_rr_index(df):
                               return df.withColumn("_rr_idx", F.row_number().over(Window.orderBy(F.monotonically_increasing_id())))
                        
                           df1_rr = add_rr_index(df_hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_in1)
                           df2_rr = add_rr_index(df_hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_in2)
                        
                           df1_rr = df1_rr.withColumn("_rr_order", (F.col("_rr_idx") - 1) * 2)  # even positions
                           df2_rr = df2_rr.withColumn("_rr_order", (F.col("_rr_idx") - 1) * 2 + 1)  # odd positions
                        
                           df_Collector_Paid_Adjs = (
                               df1_rr.unionByName(df2_rr)
                                      .orderBy("_rr_order")
                                      .drop("_rr_idx", "_rr_order")
                           )
                           ```
                        3. Keep all columns identical to the original schema and write to `hf_fcts_cap_extr_main_driver.parquet` as before.
                        4. Remove or comment out the previous simple `unionByName` implementation to avoid unintended ordering.

                    Check name:
                        Stage Variables Implementation

                    Explanation:
                        Stage variable "svAge" is not correctly translated.
                        DataStage expects AGE(svBirthDt, svErnDt) which returns the exact age in years taking months / days into account.  
                        PySpark code approximates it by simple year-difference and even applies F.year() to two string columns ("svErnDt" and "svBirthDt") which will return null.  
                        Therefore the logic does not reproduce the DataStage behaviour.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:

                        Target content:

                                "svAge",
                                F.when(
                                    F.col("Strip.DATA_SOURCE") == F.lit("CRCA"),
                                    F.lit(0)
                                ).otherwise(
                                    # AGE(svBirthDt, svErnDt) -> approximate difference in years
                                    (F.year(F.col("svErnDt")) - F.year(F.col("svBirthDt")))
                                )
                            )


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Cast the formatted strings back to date before using them.
                           svErnDtDt   = F.to_date(F.col("svErnDt"), "yyyy-MM-dd")
                           svBirthDtDt = F.to_date(F.col("svBirthDt"), "yyyy-MM-dd")
                        2. Replace the approximate year difference with correct age computation, e.g.:
                           svAge = F.when(F.col("Strip.DATA_SOURCE") == "CRCA", 0).otherwise(
                                     F.floor(F.months_between(svErnDtDt, svBirthDtDt) / 12)
                                   )
                        3. Add these intermediate columns (or inline the expression) into withColumn("svAge", …) so that exact age is produced.

                --- Item 1 ---
                    Failed check index:
                        0

                    Check name:
                        CHashedFileStage - Query fidelity for hashed file removal

                    Explanation:
                        The DataStage hashed file stage "hf_ipbd_cd_mppng" simply carries forward the output of the upstream CD_MPPNG table (columns CD_MPPNG_SK, SRC_CD, TRGT_CD). 
                        
                        In the PySpark translation, the SQL used to fetch these rows adds four extra tables (P_CAE_MBR_DRVR, MBR, SUB, CD_MPPNG) without any join conditions:
                        
                            FROM {IDSOwner}.CD_MPPNG MPPNG
                            {IDSOwner}.p_cae_mbr_drvr P_CAE_MBR_DRVR, {IDSOwner}.mbr MBR, {IDSOwner}.sub SUB, {IDSOwner}.cd_mppng CD_MPPNG
                        
                        This will either raise a syntax error (due to the missing comma after the first table) or create an unintended Cartesian product.  Either way, the returned dataset will not match the simple one-table result expected from the DataStage stage, so the PySpark code does not faithfully implement the stage logic.

                    Failure snippet:
                        Source content:
                            {"StageName": "hf_ipbd_cd_mppng", "StageType": "CHashedFileStage", "InputPins": [{"LinkIdentifier": "V339S1P1", "LinkName": "Lnk_Cd_Mppng", "InputIdentifiers": "V339S0|V339S0P1", "InputStageName": "CD_MPPNG"}], "OutputPins": [{"LinkIdentifier": "V339S1P2", "LinkName": "Cd_Mppng_lkp", "OutputIdentifiers": "V0S415|V0S415P10", "OutputStageName": "Xfm_Mbr_Lkp", "Columns": [{"ColumnName": "CD_MPPNG_SK", "SqlType": "int", "PrimaryKey": "true", "Nullable": false}, {"ColumnName": "SRC_CD", "SqlType": "varchar", "Nullable": true}, {"ColumnName": "TRGT_CD", "SqlType": "varchar", "Nullable": true}]}], "ReadFileProperties": {"FilePath": [{"directory": "", "filename": "hf_ipbd_cd_mppng"}]}}

                        Target content:
                            # CD_MPPNG (DB2) -> hf_ipbd_cd_mppng (scenario A dedup on CD_MPPNG_SK)
                            query_CD_MPPNG = f"""
                            SELECT
                            CD_MPPNG_SK,
                            SRC_CD,
                            TRGT_CD
                            FROM
                            {IDSOwner}.CD_MPPNG MPPNG
                            {IDSOwner}.p_cae_mbr_drvr P_CAE_MBR_DRVR, {IDSOwner}.mbr MBR, {IDSOwner}.sub SUB, {IDSOwner}.cd_mppng CD_MPPNG
                            """
                            df_CD_MPPNG = (
                                spark.read.format("jdbc")
                                .option("url", jdbc_url_IDS)
                                .options(**jdbc_props_IDS)
                                .option("query", query_CD_MPPNG)
                                .load()
                            )
                            df_CD_MPPNG_dedup = dedup_sort(
                                df_CD_MPPNG,
                                partition_cols=["CD_MPPNG_SK"],
                                sort_cols=[]
                            )


                    Suggested fix:
                        1. Replace the current SQL string with one that only selects from the CD_MPPNG table:
                        
                            query_CD_MPPNG = f"""
                            SELECT CD_MPPNG_SK,
                                   SRC_CD,
                                   TRGT_CD
                            FROM   {IDSOwner}.CD_MPPNG
                            """

                            df_CD_MPPNG_dedup = dedup_sort(
                                df_CD_MPPNG,
                                partition_cols=["CD_MPPNG_SK"],
                                sort_cols=[]
                            )
                        
                        3. Leave the downstream aliasing ("Cd_Mppng_lkp") and joins unchanged.
                        
                        This yields the same three-column dataset the hashed file would provide in DataStage and removes the risk of cartesian products or SQL errors.
